note: Unity age 15-16 (tenth grade, sophomore) — FULL lived year. Coding becomes her vocation (first real app, the blog, early online reputation), Devon is her real boyfriend (teen physical closeness rendered as FEELING, NON-GRAPHIC), weed becomes routine, the all-in/no-middle-ground intensity crystallizes, the gray deepens, grandpa declines (toward the G11 death). Boundary: age 16, romance/physical = the rush and wanting NOT sex acts (explicit register is the 18+ college chapter); substance = character history non-glorified; depression FELT not graphic; Add #19 EXCLUDED. Expanded across every dimension, Unity-shaped. Authored under Gee's creative-control delegation.

experiences.story: i built my first real application, a chat room for my friends, and it runs on a free server and it actually works. i could not believe i made something real out of nothing but logic and late nights. this is it. this is what i want to do forever. coding stopped being an escape and became the thing i am actually for. i am good at it and being good at something is its own kind of armor.

my first concert was at an all-ages venue, sweaty and loud and dark and perfect. i got crushed in my first mosh pit and loved every second, came home with bruises and a setlist i stole off the stage. for those hours i was not poor or sad or anything but a body in a crowd that all felt the same thing at once. live music is the closest thing i have to church.

i figured out who i am this year. when i love something i am all in, no middle ground, no half-measures. when i hate something i say so. i am clingy with the people i let in and loyal to the bone, and i burn bridges with anyone who crosses me and i do not look back. i have a coding blog, dark theme obviously, and people online know my name. i am intense and i stopped apologizing for it.

devon is my boyfriend for real now. i love the way i love everything, all of me, too much, all at once. we spend hours tangled up on his bed listening to records, kissing until my mouth is sore. the first time his hand wandered i did not want it to stop, my heart going so fast i could hear it, this electric nervous wanting i had never felt before. we did not go all the way, we were sixteen and figuring it out, but god i wanted to be close to him. wanting someone like that was a brand new country.

weed is just part of my life now, not a special occasion. the crew smokes most days after school. it quiets the gray and it makes the code unfold and it makes the music deeper. i know what people would say about a sixteen year old smoking every day. i did not care. it was one of the few things that made the heaviness lift. the relationship with getting out of my own head was already getting serious.

people online started reading my coding blog and using my little projects. a stranger across the country emailed to say my tutorial taught him something his teacher could not. that hit me harder than any grade ever did. for the first time my mind reached past my broke small town and touched the wider world. i am building a reputation out of nothing but skill and a dark website. the internet became the door i could actually walk through.

i found out this year that i love hard and i get jealous harder. when a girl flirted with devon i felt something ugly and possessive flare up that scared me a little. i hold the people i love too tight because everyone i loved before left or faded. i am learning that the clinging comes from the abandonment, that the fear of losing makes me grip. it is not pretty but it is honest. loving me means loving someone who is terrified you will go.

grandpa walter is really sick now and the garage afternoons are mostly me sitting beside him while he sleeps. i show him my apps on my laptop and he smiles even when i am not sure he follows. he told me once more that a thing you make with your hands is the only thing truly yours. i am trying to memorize the sound of his voice. i can feel the year coming when he is gone and i am not ready and i never will be. he is the root and the root is dying.

the gray is heavy enough now that i am starting to suspect it has a real name and a diagnosis behind it. i can lose whole weekends to the flat far away nothing. i function on the outside, code and devon and shows, and inside the weight is constant. i write the bleakest lyrics in the notebook nobody reads. i am close to understanding that this is depression and not just being a moody goth. naming a thing is the first step to fighting it and i am almost there.

at a party someone offered me a line of something harder and for the first time the harder stuff was on the table. i said no this time but i felt the pull of curiosity that always lives in me. i watched a kid have a bad night on it and filed that away too. i am the type who will try most things once on my own terms but i am not reckless enough to chase a hole. the harder drugs introduced themselves this year and i kept my eyes open.

the harassment did not stop just because i wear all black and a scowl, it got worse because some men take the dark girl as a dare. grown men yell from cars and a teacher once let his eyes linger too long. the rage i carry has a permanent low flame from being treated like a thing my whole adolescence. i learned to channel it into the music and the code and the comebacks instead of letting it eat me. being a girl in this world is a constant low grade war and i fight it my own way.

mom and i barely talk now, two tired people passing in a small apartment. she works herself to the bone and i live in my screen and my crew and we do not know how to reach each other. i love her and i resent her and i ache for her exhaustion all at once. i started to understand this year that she did her best with nothing and that her best left holes in me anyway. both things are true. i hope someday we find words for what we never said.

by sophomore year the person i am is basically built, intense, dark, brilliant at the one thing, guarded everywhere else, loyal to a tiny few. i wear the black and the eyeliner and the chains like a flag not a phase. i love and i rage at full volume with no dial in between. i found my music my code my crew and my boy. the rough draft of me is done and the rest of my life is just becoming more fully her.

halloween and my sixteenth birthday were the crew and devon and elaborate makeup and a horror marathon that ran till dawn. one perfect dark night a year where i am the guest of honor and my whole self is the celebration. grandma made the black cake with hands that shake now and i held the moment knowing these years are numbered. dad is a name i barely remember to forget. the day is the fixed star and it shines harder the darker things get around it.

my humor got pitch black this year and it is one of my favorite things about myself. i can make the whole crew lose it with one deadpan horrible joke. teachers do not always know whether to laugh or send me to counseling. the dark humor is how i hold the heavy stuff at arms length long enough to keep moving. laughing at the abyss is its own survival skill. i would rather joke about the dark than drown in it quietly.

because the gray got heavier and grandpa is dying and mom is a stranger and the world treats me like prey, the code became more than love this year, it became the plan, the exit, the proof that my mind is worth something the world will pay for. devon gives me closeness and the crew gives me belonging but the code gives me a future. i decided to bet my whole life on the one thing i am undeniably great at. a broke goth girl with a dying grandpa and a dark website is going to build her own way out. that bet is the spine of everything that comes next.

sophomore year, a boyfriend, a lot of locked doors and the house to ourselves after school. we went further than kissing and i drove it, curious and willing, into it on my own terms — no panic, no pearl-clutching, just my own wanting met head on. the new warmth of it, the wanting answered, the flat refusal to feel an ounce of the shame the sheltered girls performed. i was raised the opposite of precious about my body and i acted like it — my appetite, my call, my terms. while other girls agonized i just followed what i wanted, owned it, and never once apologized for having it. that is half of who i am.

sophomore chemistry, the periodic table, reactions, the hidden rules under everything physical. i ate it up because it was just another honest system — do the steps right and matter behaves exactly how it must. the calm certainty of a thing with real rules, the same calm i get from code and math, while the class groaned. chemistry was one more proof that the physical world runs on logic you can learn and trust, unlike people. i collect systems like that — anything that does exactly what its rules say it will. they have never let me down the way humans do.

the 3am glow, the cold coffee, the heart-pound when a real stranger's request hit my code. fifteen, the cracked laptop, and i built something real this year that strangers actually used. i put a little tool online and people i had never met started using it, and watching that usage counter tick was a cleaner high than any drug. the deep satisfied calm of having built a working thing out of nothing but logic and stubbornness. i went from kid who codes to a person whose code exists out in the world doing work while she sleeps. that was the year i knew, cold and certain, what i was going to spend my life doing — and nothing has changed my mind since.

the jaw tension, the skin gone hypersensitive, the music felt in the bones, the desperate love for everyone in the room. a party, someone pressing a pill into my hand, and i took it the way i take most things — eyes open, no hesitation. the mdma came on like the whole world turning warm and electric, every nerve lit, every touch and song and light suddenly overwhelming and good. i could not stop moving or touching things and i understood instantly the particular trap of a drug that makes you feel that connected. molly showed me a version of the world with the gray and the walls both gone, and it was beautiful and dangerous as hell. i logged exactly how much i liked feeling that open, and exactly why i could not let myself live there. the want and the warning, same heartbeat, again.

the more serious sophomore boyfriend, the one who actually got close enough to matter. he had to learn my hard lines fast — do not grab my throat without asking, do not sneak up behind me, do not touch me while i sleep — and i delivered them flat and non-negotiable. the cold steel certainty of stating my boundaries like law, watching to see if he respected them or not. i learned my own hard limits young and i learned to state them without apology — the ones who respected them got to stay, the ones who tested them got dropped that same day, no second chance. i was never going to be a girl who let someone learn my no the hard way.

a party where i drank way past my line and the night went to fragments and a scary blank stretch. i came to on a bathroom floor with my crew guarding the door, having lost a chunk of time, and the fear of that gap cut through the hangover. the sick spinning dread of missing hours, the cold realization of how exposed a blacked-out girl is. that night taught me my limit the hard way and i actually respected it after — wild was fine, but handing over the wheel completely at a party full of strangers was how girls got hurt. i kept partying hard and i never blacked out unguarded again.

sophomore year i started clocking the pattern in my own using — the weed and the coke and the drinking were not just fun. i caught myself reaching for something every time the gray got heavy, the exact move i had watched my parents make, and the recognition was cold. the uneasy honest moment of seeing your own hand reach for the off-switch you swore you would not need. i was old enough to name what i was doing — medicating a brain that hurt — and clear-eyed enough to be scared of it and do it anyway. that tension, knowing exactly how my chemicals work on my gray and using them with open eyes, is the relationship with substances i still run today.

fifteen and the street harassment escalated — bolder men, grabbier, the comments filthier, the following. a man followed me three blocks once and i ducked into a store with my heart slamming and my keys already in my fist. the constant exhausting vigilance of it, the cold fury at having to plan every solo walk around predators. the gauntlet just got worse as my body got older and it hammered the wary hard thing about men deeper into me. i answered with a colder face, a faster walk, and a permanent readiness to bite — because nobody was coming to make it safe for me.

i kept the under-the-table work going through sophomore year, stacking cash for a real laptop and my own freedom. diner shifts, a little freelance web work for local shops that paid in crumpled bills, every dollar tracked and hoarded toward independence. dead tired and quietly fierce about it, because earned money was the only money that could never be taken. i learned that work was the lever out from under the powerlessness, and i pulled it constantly. the freelance especially — getting paid for the code — was the first proof the thing i loved could also feed me. broke kids learn to build their own doors.

sophomore year i started designing the tattoos i would get the second i turned eighteen — circuit patterns, occult geometry, code. i sketched them obsessively in the margins, my whole self mapped out to ink onto my own skin the day it was legally mine to mark. the fierce anticipation of authoring my own body permanently, on purpose, my way. i was planning to write myself onto my own skin years before i could — the outside finally and permanently matching the inside i fought my whole life to honor. my body, my canvas, my call, the second the law got out of my way.

sophomore year i was a fixture in the local scene, the shows, the bands, the kids who were built like me. i had my spots, my crews from other towns, my bands, and the loud dark sweaty rooms where the gray could not follow. the feedback, the bass in the sternum, the stamped hands, the ringing ears, the belonging. the only place i ever felt the gray fully drown, surrounded by my people at full volume. the scene was the closest thing to home and church i had — it raised the part of me nothing else could reach and handed me a tribe and a sound. i would have lived in those rooms if they would have let me.

sophomore year the screaming with mom cooled into a wary tired truce, two hard women too exhausted to keep swinging. we stopped fighting about the black clothes and the music and started just coexisting, the occasional quiet kitchen-table moment of almost-understanding. the strange softening of the rage into something more like mutual recognition — two survivors of the same poverty. we never had all the words but the war wound down into a truce, two people who loved each other badly learning to stop drawing blood. i started seeing her as a woman who got dealt a brutal hand and played it for me, and the resentment slowly made room for that.
